content.text: Support SLA Definition and Tracking

A Service Level Agreement (SLA) for support defines the minimum response and resolution times a customer can expect when they report an issue. For GenAI platforms, SLA definitions need careful calibration: the failure modes of AI systems (quality regressions, safety events, probabilistic failures) do not map cleanly onto the SLA structures designed for deterministic software, where bugs are either reproducible or not and fixes can be precisely scheduled.

An SLA that is too aggressive creates operational debt — the support team burns capacity meeting response time commitments on issues that require longer investigation. An SLA that is too permissive fails enterprise customers who need reliability guarantees. Getting SLA definition right requires understanding what can realistically be committed to, what drives customer trust, and how GenAI-specific failure types affect the ability to meet conventional SLA targets.

attrs.alt: Support SLA tiers by customer tier: response and resolution targets for GenAI platforms

content.text: SLA Dimensions
Support SLAs for GenAI platforms have three distinct dimensions, each measuring a different aspect of support quality:
First Response Time

First response time (FRT) is the time from ticket creation to the first substantive response from a support engineer. 'Substantive' means a response that acknowledges the specific issue, not a generic auto-acknowledgement. FRT is the most operationally measurable SLA dimension and the one customers notice first.

For GenAI quality issues, FRT is complicated by the evidence collection requirement: a first response that says 'we are investigating' without understanding the issue is less useful than a first response that requests the exact input/output and explains next steps. Structure the FRT measurement to count from when evidence is received, not from when the ticket is created, for quality complaints.
Time to Resolution
Time to resolution (TTR) is the time from ticket creation to the issue being closed as resolved. TTR for GenAI issues varies enormously by type:
Issue type
Typical TTR range
TTR driver
Configuration error (customer-side)
Hours
L2 identifies and guides customer to fix
Known limitation (documented in KB)
Hours
L2 explains and links workaround
Infrastructure bug (reproducible, clear cause)
Hours to days
L3 identifies fix; deployment pipeline determines timing
Model quality regression (known cause)
Days to weeks
ML team investigation and model update
Model hallucination (probabilistic, narrow domain)
Weeks to months
Fine-tuning or retrieval augmentation required
Safety-related model behavior
Hours for mitigation; longer for systemic fix
Safety team approves mitigation; model fix requires ML review

Because TTR varies so widely for GenAI issues, avoid committing to a fixed resolution time for quality issues in customer SLAs. Instead, commit to  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'investigation update frequency', 'marks': [{'type': 'italic'}]}  — the customer will receive a status update every N hours while the issue is under investigation, even if there is no resolution yet.
Uptime / Availability
Uptime SLAs define the percentage of time the platform is available for API calls. This is measured at the platform level, not per-customer. Standard uptime targets:
{'type': 'text', 'text': '99.9% monthly uptime', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  = 43.8 minutes of allowed downtime per month. Appropriate for enterprise tiers.
{'type': 'text', 'text': '99.5% monthly uptime', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  = 3.65 hours of allowed downtime per month. Professional tier.
{'type': 'text', 'text': '99.0% monthly uptime', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  = 7.3 hours of allowed downtime per month. Startup or high-volume self-serve.

GenAI-specific caveat: uptime SLAs typically cover API availability (whether the endpoint accepts requests), not output quality. A platform can be '100% available' while producing degraded model output due to a quality regression. Consider whether your SLA covers quality availability as well — if so, you need automated quality monitoring to track it.
Tiered SLA Structure
Most GenAI platforms offer tiered support with SLAs that scale with the customer's contract value and criticality. A four-tier structure is common:
Enterprise Tier

Enterprise customers pay for guaranteed response times, 24/7 coverage, dedicated account management, and uptime commitments backed by service credits. For GenAI platforms, enterprise SLAs should include:
30-minute first response for P1 (safety events, complete outage) — 24/7
2-hour first response for P2 — 24/7
4-hour resolution target for P1 (or workaround provided within 4 hours if fix takes longer)
Status updates every 1 hour during active P1 incidents
99.9% monthly uptime guarantee with service credit formula
Dedicated account manager with direct escalation path to L3
Quarterly business review including support quality metrics
Professional Tier
Professional tier customers have significant usage but may not require full 24/7 coverage. Key differences from enterprise:
1-hour first response for P1 during extended business hours (e.g., 06:00–22:00 local time)
24/7 pager for confirmed outages (pager activates for infrastructure issues, not quality complaints)
Quality complaints: next business day acknowledgement
99.5% monthly uptime commitment
Startup Tier
Startup tier customers typically have best-effort quality complaint handling but guaranteed infrastructure issue response:
4-hour first response for P1 infrastructure issues during business hours
Quality complaints: 24-hour acknowledgement
99.0% monthly uptime target (no contractual guarantee)
No dedicated account manager; shared support queue
Self-Serve Tier
Self-serve customers use community resources, documentation, and async email support. No contractual SLA. Support is best-effort with email response targeting 1–3 business days.
What SLAs Should Not Cover for GenAI

Committing to SLAs that cannot be reliably met is worse than having no SLA — missed commitments erode trust faster than absent commitments. Several GenAI-specific areas should be explicitly excluded from SLA scope or handled with careful language:
Area
SLA risk
Recommended approach
Model output quality (specific domains)
Quality is probabilistic; cannot guarantee specific outputs
Commit to investigating quality complaints within SLA, not to fixing them within SLA
Hallucination rate
Rate depends on use case and prompt; cannot be universally guaranteed
Offer eval suite tooling; customers measure their own use-case quality
Response latency for large contexts
P99 latency for 100k token contexts varies with load
Publish P50/P95/P99 latency targets as guidelines, not contractual commitments
Model regression fixes
Fix timeline depends on ML team investigation; outside standard sprint
Commit to communication cadence (updates every 48h), not to fix timeline
Safety filter accuracy (false positive rate)
Filter tuning involves model-level changes; accuracy cannot be contractually guaranteed
Commit to investigating false positives within SLA; not to zero false positives
SLA Tracking Implementation
SLA tracking requires instrumentation in the ticketing system that records timestamps, applies priority, and alerts when tickets approach their SLA deadline.
SLA Clock Management
The SLA clock measures elapsed time between defined events. Key decisions in clock management:

# SLA clock rules — configure in ticketing system (Zendesk, Jira, Salesforce)

class SLAClock:
    """
    Tracks time-to-first-response and time-to-resolution per ticket.
    """

    def clock_start(self, ticket):
        # Clock starts when ticket is CREATED by customer
        # For quality complaints: consider starting clock when
        # evidence is confirmed received (L1 marks as 'evidence complete')
        return ticket.created_at

    def clock_pause(self, ticket, event):
        # Pause the clock when:
        # 1. Waiting for customer response ('needs-info' status)
        # 2. Outside business hours (for business-hours SLAs only)
        # 3. Waiting for customer to implement a workaround and confirm
        if event == "status_changed_to_needs_info":
            return True
        if self.is_business_hours_sla(ticket) and not self.is_business_hours():
            return True
        return False

    def clock_resume(self, ticket, event):
        # Resume when:
        # 1. Customer responds (needs-info resolved)
        # 2. Business hours resume
        if event == "customer_replied":
            return True
        return False

    def sla_breached(self, ticket):
        elapsed = self.elapsed_active_time(ticket)
        target = self.get_sla_target(ticket.priority, ticket.customer_tier)
        return elapsed > target

    def get_sla_target(self, priority, tier):
        targets = {
            ("P1", "enterprise"): timedelta(minutes=30),
            ("P1", "professional"): timedelta(hours=1),
            ("P1", "startup"): timedelta(hours=4),
            ("P2", "enterprise"): timedelta(hours=2),
            ("P2", "professional"): timedelta(hours=4),
            ("P2", "startup"): timedelta(hours=8),
            ("P2", "self_serve"): timedelta(hours=24),
        }
        return targets.get((priority, tier), timedelta(hours=48))
SLA Breach Alerting
The ticketing system should send alerts at two points:
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'At 75% of SLA target elapsed time', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — warning to the assigned engineer and their manager. There is still time to respond before breach.

{'type': 'text', 'text': 'At 100% of SLA target elapsed time', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — breach alert to the engineer, manager, and account manager (for enterprise customers). Breach must be acknowledged within 15 minutes.

Breach alerts that go unacknowledged for 15 minutes escalate to the engineering manager and director level. The escalation chain ensures that SLA breaches are visible at the right level of management to either resolve the ticket or begin service credit calculations.
SLA Reporting
SLA attainment is reported externally (to enterprise customers) and internally (to leadership). The two reports have different audiences and granularities:
Customer-Facing SLA Report
Enterprise customers receive a monthly SLA attainment report as part of their account management relationship. The report covers:
Overall SLA attainment for the month (% of tickets resolved within SLA)
Attainment broken down by priority (P1, P2) and metric (FRT, TTR)
Uptime attainment vs. guarantee
Any SLA breaches: ticket IDs, breach duration, and root cause
Service credits due if breaches triggered the credit formula
Customer-facing reports use the contracted SLA targets as the baseline. Internal reports may use tighter internal targets to provide buffer before contractual breach.
Internal SLA Dashboard
The internal SLA dashboard is updated in real time and reviewed in the weekly triage meeting. Key views:
Dashboard view
Content
Used for
Live SLA status
All open tickets with current SLA time remaining; color-coded by risk (green/yellow/red)
Daily operational monitoring
Weekly attainment trend
SLA attainment % per week, last 12 weeks, broken down by tier and priority
Identifying deteriorating trends before they become customer-visible
Breach root cause log
All breaches this month with root cause (staffing gap, investigation complexity, missing evidence)
Process improvement; capacity planning
Near-miss rate
Tickets resolved within 10% of SLA target — early warning of capacity pressure
Proactive staffing adjustments
Service Credits for SLA Breaches
Enterprise contracts typically include a service credit formula — a financial remedy for SLA breaches. Defining this clearly upfront prevents disputes and aligns incentives:

SERVICE CREDIT FORMULA (example — adjust to your pricing)

Uptime SLA (99.9% monthly):
  Downtime 0–1h beyond SLA:    5% credit of monthly fee
  Downtime 1–4h beyond SLA:   10% credit of monthly fee
  Downtime > 4h beyond SLA:   25% credit of monthly fee
  Maximum monthly credit:      50% of monthly fee

Support Response SLA (P1 first response):
  Single breach per month:     No credit (grace breach)
  2–3 breaches per month:      5% credit per breach
  4+ breaches per month:      10% credit per breach
  Maximum monthly credit:      25% of monthly support fee

Exclusions from SLA calculations:
  - Scheduled maintenance (30 days notice)
  - Customer-caused outages (misconfiguration, traffic above agreed limits)
  - Force majeure events
  - Requests for which customer did not provide required evidence within 24h
  - Beta features explicitly excluded from SLA coverage

Exclusions are as important as the credit formula. The 'customer did not provide required evidence' exclusion prevents a situation where a quality complaint SLA clock runs while L2 is waiting for the customer to share their input/output. The SLA clock should pause during customer-pending states.
GenAI-Specific SLA Complications
Several properties of AI systems create SLA complications that do not arise in traditional software support. Understanding these upfront prevents contractual commitments that cannot be honored.
Safety Events and SLA Priority

Safety events require a priority tier that sits above the standard P1/P2 classification. A safety event — confirmed harmful content generated and delivered to a user — needs a faster response than a complete API outage, because the potential harm is not a service interruption but a trust and safety violation.
Define a P0 or Safety-P1 category in the SLA with separate commitments:

SAFETY-P1 SLA COMMITMENTS (Enterprise tier example)
=====================================================
Trigger: Confirmed safety policy violation or suspected data breach
         reported by customer OR detected by internal monitoring

Response commitments:
  - AI Safety team acknowledgement: within 15 minutes (24/7)
  - Initial investigation complete: within 1 hour
  - Customer communication sent: within 30 minutes of report receipt
  - Status update cadence: every 30 minutes until resolved

Note on this SLA: it is separate from P1 infrastructure SLA.
Both can be active simultaneously (e.g., safety event during an outage).
Safety-P1 takes priority on communication and resource allocation.

Service credits for Safety-P1 breach: not applicable —
safety events trigger separate remediation process, not credits.

Safety-P1 is not in the standard credit formula because safety events require a different remediation approach (disclosure, policy review, filter update) that cannot be reduced to a credit against a monthly fee.
Quality SLAs: Investigation vs. Resolution

The most common SLA design mistake for GenAI platforms is committing to a quality issue  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'resolution', 'marks': [{'type': 'italic'}]}  SLA. Quality issues (model regressions, hallucinations, output degradation) may require ML engineering investigation and model updates that take weeks. The correct commitment is on the  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'investigation', 'marks': [{'type': 'italic'}]}  side:
SLA type
Can commit to
Cannot commit to
First response
Yes — acknowledge within N hours
Initial investigation
Yes — L2 investigates within N hours of evidence receipt
Root cause identification
Yes — provide root cause or update within N business days
Not always possible if issue is probabilistic
Fix deployment
No — depends on ML team and deployment pipeline
Avoid committing to specific fix dates for quality issues
Fix in next model version
Conditional — only if ML team confirms it is in scope
Avoid unless engineering explicitly commits

Communicate this distinction clearly to enterprise customers at contract time. A customer who believes their quality complaint will be resolved in 4 hours (like an infrastructure issue) will be frustrated when L2 says the investigation will take 2 weeks. Set expectations correctly upfront.
Uptime SLA Exclusions for AI Infrastructure
AI platforms have infrastructure components that are harder to guarantee than stateless API servers. Be specific about what is and is not covered by uptime SLAs:
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Covered:', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  API endpoint availability — the endpoint accepts requests and returns responses
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Covered:', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  Authentication and key management system availability
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Not covered:', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  Model output quality (a response can be returned without meeting quality standards)
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Not covered:', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  Response latency — latency can degrade significantly without the endpoint being 'down'
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Not covered:', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  Specific model version availability — the platform may switch to a different version during a regression
{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Typically excluded:', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  GPU-dependent features during hardware capacity constraints — add explicit language if your platform has GPU queuing
SLA Negotiation with Enterprise Customers
Enterprise customers frequently negotiate SLA terms beyond the standard tier. A principled approach to SLA negotiation prevents committing to terms that create operational unsustainability:
What Is Negotiable
Response time targets — can be tightened if engineering capacity supports it
Coverage hours — can be extended from extended business hours to 24/7 for an additional fee
Dedicated support contact — a named engineer who knows the customer's deployment
Custom escalation path — direct pager to a specific L3 engineer or team for this customer's tier
SLA reporting frequency — monthly by default, but weekly reports are possible for large contracts
Service credit formula — the percentage per breach or the cap can be adjusted
What Is Not Negotiable
Model output quality commitments — the model is probabilistic; no individual customer gets a quality guarantee beyond what is achievable for all customers
Fix timelines for model-level issues — ML team engineering timelines are set by technical requirements, not by customer contracts
Safety review timelines — safety decisions are made by the AI Safety team, not by the support team or account management
Uptime guarantees above 99.9% — achieving five-nines for AI inference infrastructure requires architectural investment that cannot be contractually unlocked for individual customers

When a customer asks for a commitment that cannot be met, the honest answer is better than a yes that creates a breach. 'We cannot commit to X, but we can commit to Y' preserves trust more than over-promising and missing.
Summary

Support SLA definition for GenAI platforms requires adapting conventional SLA structures to accommodate AI-specific failure modes. Three SLA dimensions apply: first response time (measurable within hours), time to resolution (highly variable by issue type — commit to update frequency, not fixed resolution time), and uptime availability (platform-level, not quality-level). A four-tier structure (Enterprise/Professional/Startup/Self-Serve) maps customer contract value to support commitments. Several areas should be explicitly excluded from SLA commitments: model output quality, hallucination rates, model regression fix timelines, and safety filter accuracy — these are commitments that cannot be reliably met. SLA tracking requires clock management rules (pause during customer-pending, outside business hours for business-hours SLAs), breach alerting at 75% and 100% of target, and an internal dashboard with real-time SLA status. External SLA reports go to enterprise customers monthly; service credits apply per a defined formula with explicit exclusions.
Key takeaways:
Three SLA dimensions: first response time, time to resolution (commit to update frequency, not fixed TTR), and uptime
GenAI SLAs should NOT commit to quality fix timelines, hallucination rates, or safety filter accuracy — these cannot be reliably met
SLA clocks pause during customer-pending states (waiting for evidence) and outside business hours for business-hours tiers
Alert at 75% of target (warning) and 100% (breach) — unacknowledged breach alerts escalate to management within 15 minutes
Enterprise customers receive monthly SLA attainment reports; service credits apply per formula with defined exclusions
Internal targets should be tighter than contractual commitments to provide buffer before breach
